title: Merck — Oncology — Knowledge Doc
html:
Market Context: KEYTRUDA (pembrolizumab) in Oncology

Product: KEYTRUDA (pembrolizumab) + KEYTRUDA QLEX (pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph) Manufacturer: Merck & Co., Inc., Rahway, N.J., USA Indication: Broad oncology portfolio — 40+ approved indications across solid tumors and hematologic malignancies Document scope: Product-level market context, competitive landscape, commercial dynamics, and LOE strategy Last updated: April 2026

1. Disease Landscape & Product Overview

1.1 KEYTRUDA Overview

KEYTRUDA (pembrolizumab) is a humanized monoclonal antibody that blocks the interaction between PD-1 and its ligands PD-L1 and PD-L2, reactivating the immune system's ability to detect and destroy cancer cells. Since its initial accelerated approval in 2014 for metastatic melanoma, KEYTRUDA has become the world's top-selling oncology drug, with ~$31.7 billion in 2025 global sales.

KEYTRUDA is now approved in 40+ indications across more than 20 tumor types, including:

  • Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC): The largest revenue driver (~40% of Keytruda sales). Approved as monotherapy and in combination with chemotherapy across 1L metastatic, neoadjuvant/adjuvant (KEYNOTE-671), and locally advanced settings
  • Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC): Recurrent/metastatic (KEYNOTE-048) and newly approved perioperative locally advanced (KEYNOTE-689)
  • Melanoma: Adjuvant and metastatic
  • Urothelial carcinoma: Perioperative (with enfortumab vedotin/Padcev) and metastatic
  • Renal cell carcinoma: First-line with Inlyta (axitinib)
  • Endometrial carcinoma: With Lenvima (lenvatinib)
  • Cervical cancer, gastric/GEJ, esophageal, hepatocellular, biliary tract, TNBC, MSI-H/dMMR tumors, and others

1.2 Key Indications and Clinical Context

NSCLC — The Keytruda Anchor

NSCLC accounts for approximately 85% of all lung cancers, with ~230,000 new cases annually in the US.

  • 1L Metastatic: KEYTRUDA + chemotherapy (KEYNOTE-189 for nonsquamous; KEYNOTE-407 for squamous) is standard of care for PD-L1-expressing tumors. KEYTRUDA monotherapy is first-line for PD-L1 TPS >=50%
  • Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant: KEYNOTE-671 demonstrated EFS benefit for perioperative pembrolizumab + chemotherapy in resectable Stage II-IIIB NSCLC — approved March 2024
  • ZoomRx study insight: Pulmonologists and surgeons frequently defer IO discussion to oncologists. Only ~33% of conversations mention neoadjuvant treatment. Patients focus on immediate surgery, not long-term treatment planning. MDs communicate at a 5th-grade reading level, and patients struggle to retain information

HNSCC — The New Frontier (KEYNOTE-689)

Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma has ~66,000 new cases annually in the US, with locally advanced (Stage III-IVA) resectable disease representing a significant treatment opportunity.

  • KEYNOTE-689: Phase 3 trial in 714 patients with resectable LA HNSCC (Stage III-IVA). Pembrolizumab as neoadjuvant treatment, continued as adjuvant with radiation +/- cisplatin, then as single agent
    • Primary endpoint (EFS): Median 51.8 months (pembro) vs. 30.4 months (SOC); HR 0.70
    • Key secondary endpoints: pCR ~5-10%, mPR ~10-20%
    • Biomarker requirement: PD-L1 CPS >=1 via IHC
    • FDA approved: June 12, 2025 — first perioperative approval in LA HNSCC and first HNSCC approval in 6 years
  • KEYNOTE-048 (R/M HNSCC): Pembrolizumab + chemotherapy or monotherapy (CPS >=1) for recurrent/metastatic — established IO standard of care since 2019

Urothelial Carcinoma — Padcev Combination

  • KEYNOTE-A39/EV-302: Pembrolizumab + enfortumab vedotin (Padcev) approved as 1L treatment for locally advanced or metastatic urothelial cancer. Also approved perioperatively for cisplatin-ineligible MIBC
  • This combination represents one of Keytruda's fastest-growing new indications

Ovarian Cancer — New Entry (2026)

  • February 2026: FDA approved KEYTRUDA and KEYTRUDA QLEX + paclitaxel +/- bevacizumab for PD-L1+ (CPS >=1) platinum-resistant epithelial ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal carcinoma — first and only PD-1 inhibitor approved in this setting

1.3 Subcutaneous Formulation: KEYTRUDA QLEX

KEYTRUDA QLEX (pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph) is the first and only subcutaneously administered immune checkpoint inhibitor:

  • Approved: September 2025 for adults across most solid tumor indications approved for KEYTRUDA
  • Administration: Can be given by a healthcare provider in as little as 1 minute vs. 30-minute IV infusion
  • Commercial launch: Late September 2025; Q4 2025 sales of $35 million ($40 million cumulative for 2025)
  • J-code: Permanent HCPCS code J9277 effective April 1, 2026, replacing temporary J9999 — this is expected to significantly accelerate adoption
  • Adoption target: Merck expects 30-40% of Keytruda patients to transition to QLEX by 2028
  • Strategic importance: QLEX is central to Merck's LOE defense strategy — the subcutaneous formulation carries its own patent estate, potentially shielding the franchise from IV biosimilar competition for years beyond 2028

2. Competitive Landscape

2.1 IO Checkpoint Inhibitor Competitors

Product Company Mechanism Key Indications 2025 Revenue (est.) Key Differentiator
KEYTRUDA Merck Anti-PD-1 40+ indications ~$31.7B Broadest label, SubQ formulation
Opdivo (nivolumab) BMS Anti-PD-1 Melanoma, RCC, NSCLC, HCC, urothelial, etc. ~$9.5B CheckMate combinations, Opdualag (nivolumab+relatlimab)
Tecentriq (atezolizumab) Roche Anti-PD-L1 NSCLC, SCLC, HCC, urothelial ~$3.5B PD-L1 rather than PD-1 targeting
Imfinzi (durvalumab) AstraZeneca Anti-PD-L1 Stage III NSCLC, SCLC, biliary tract, endometrial ~$4.5B Strongest in unresectable Stage III NSCLC (PACIFIC)
Bavencio (avelumab) EMD Serono/Pfizer Anti-PD-L1 1L mUC maintenance ~$0.7B Niche maintenance bladder cancer
Libtayo (cemiplimab) Regeneron/Sanofi Anti-PD-1 CSCC, BCC, NSCLC, cervical ~$1B First IO for cutaneous SCC

2.2 HNSCC-Specific Competitive Landscape

In locally advanced HNSCC (the KEYNOTE-689 setting), KEYTRUDA is currently the only approved perioperative IO:

  • Opdivo (CheckMate-906/NICO-RT): BMS is running NICO-RT trial evaluating nivolumab perioperatively in resectable HNSCC — results expected 2026-2027. If positive, this would be the most direct competitive threat
  • Tecentriq: Limited HNSCC development; focus on IMbrave050 (HCC) and lung
  • Imfinzi: AstraZeneca exploring durvalumab in earlier-stage HNSCC through ORCA-5/KESTREL follow-ons, but no clear perioperative program
  • No other approved perioperative IO in LA HNSCC — KEYTRUDA has first-mover advantage

In recurrent/metastatic HNSCC (KEYNOTE-048 setting):

  • Opdivo is approved as monotherapy in platinum-refractory R/M HNSCC (CheckMate-141)
  • Keytruda dominates 1L R/M with KEYNOTE-048 data (PD-L1 CPS >=1: pembro+chemo or pembro mono)

2.3 Biosimilar Competitive Landscape

Keytruda's composition-of-matter patent expires in 2028 in the US, opening the door for biosimilar entry:

  • Active biosimilar programs: Multiple companies including Samsung Bioepis, Biocon, Coherus, and others have pembrolizumab biosimilars in development
  • Expected biosimilar launch: Late 2028 to 2029 for IV formulations
  • Merck's patent estate defense: 50+ active US patents covering methods of use, formulations, and dosing regimens could protect aspects of Keytruda through 2042
  • QLEX as competitive moat: The subcutaneous formulation carries separate patents, and switching patients to QLEX before IV biosimilars enter creates a product-hop defense
  • Historical precedent: Biologics typically see 70-80% revenue erosion within 2-3 years of biosimilar entry, but Keytruda's breadth of indications and QLEX formulation may moderate this

3. Commercial Dynamics

3.1 Revenue Trajectory

Period Keytruda Revenue Growth Key Drivers
FY 2023 ~$25.0B +19% Metastatic expansion, global growth
FY 2024 ~$29.5B +18% KEYNOTE-671 launch, new indications
Q3 2025 $8.1B +10% YoY First quarter >$8B; earlier-stage uptake, QLEX approval
Q4 2025 $8.4B +5% YoY QLEX launch ($35M in quarter), KN-689 post-approval ramp
FY 2025 ~$31.7B +7% QLEX $40M total; earlier-stage cancers; women's cancers growing
Q1 2026 ~$7.7B (est.) TBD J-code for QLEX effective April 1; ovarian cancer approval

3.2 Growth Drivers

  1. Earlier-stage cancers: Perioperative NSCLC (KN-671) and HNSCC (KN-689) are adding new patient pools that previously were surgery-only
  2. Women's cancers: Cervical (KN-826), endometrial (KN-775), and newly approved ovarian (KN-100/KEYLYNK-001) are a major growth vector
  3. Subcutaneous QLEX: Convenience and time savings expand the addressable treatment population, particularly in community settings
  4. Combination expansion: Padcev+Keytruda in urothelial, Lenvima+Keytruda in endometrial/HCC, and ongoing combination studies across tumor types
  5. Adjuvant/neoadjuvant shift: Moving IO treatment earlier in the disease course expands the total addressable market by 30-50% per tumor type

3.3 Headwinds

  1. LOE in 2028: The single largest risk to Merck's revenue base — Keytruda is >50% of pharmaceutical revenue
  2. Competitive IO market: Opdivo combinations, Imfinzi's PACIFIC franchise, and emerging bispecifics create pressure
  3. Pricing pressure: IRA drug price negotiation (Keytruda selected for negotiation); international reference pricing
  4. Tolerability in perioperative settings: Adding IO to curative-intent treatment raises the bar for benefit/risk — adverse events in surgical patients are closely scrutinized
  5. PD-L1 testing requirement (KN-689): CPS >=1 biomarker testing adds logistical complexity, especially in community settings where reflexive testing is not yet standard

4. ZoomRx Research Insights

4.1 Studies Analyzed

Study Period Type Key Focus
KN-689 HCP-Patient Conversations + Qualitative (Final Report v4) Aug 2025 - Feb 2026 HCP-Pt Dialogue + Qual KN-689 launch dynamics: PD-L1 testing, MDT discussions, KN-689 perceptions
Keytruda NSCLC HCP-Patient Study (vF) Nov 2024 - Feb 2025 HCP-Pt Dialogue + Qual Neoadjuvant referral patterns, Pulm/Surg→Onc pathway
LA HNSCC Biomarker Testing Qualitative Research (v7.0 FINAL) Dec 2024 - Jan 2025 Qualitative TDIs PD-L1 testing journey, stakeholder roles, CPS requirement impact
HNSCC LA Baseline ATU W1 (Pre-launch) Mar 2025 Quantitative Survey Pre-launch awareness, treatment patterns, MDT dynamics (n=134)
Solid Tumor SubQ IO HCP-Patient Conversations Kickoff Nov 2025 Kickoff Deck SubQ IO positioning, QLEX launch monitoring
KN-689 Listening Sessions (multiple waves) Sep 2025 - Apr 2026 Listening Sessions Ongoing HCP-Pt conversation collection

4.2 Key Research Themes

Theme 1: PD-L1 Testing Is the Adoption Gatekeeper for KN-689

  • Only 4/13 practices have formalized reflexive PD-L1 testing; 6/13 are "moving toward" it; 3/13 still manual
  • When ordered manually, PD-L1 tests are ordered by Med Oncs, not surgeons — creating a dependency
  • FNA biopsy (20-45% of HNSCC patients) yields inadequate tissue for PD-L1 in 5-15% of cases, requiring re-biopsy (1-3+ weeks delay)
  • NCCN guidelines are the strongest driver for reflexive testing adoption

Theme 2: Surgeons Are the Referral Bottleneck

  • In NSCLC, 53% of conversations do not mention MDTs or tumor boards; 27% mention surgery as curative
  • In HNSCC, surgeons own the MDT presentation but rely on Med Oncs for NAT IO decisions
  • Community surgeons are more reluctant to adopt NAT IO — one community surgeon "not yet convinced by KN689 data, awaiting input from academic peers"

Theme 3: KN-689 Post-Launch Experience Is Positive

  • Most HCPs report positive KN-689 experience: improved surgical margins, reduced surgical burden, favorable tolerability
  • Key drivers: lower tumor burden (n=11), lower recurrence risk (n=11), FDA/NCCN inclusion (n=4)
  • Key barriers: insurance/access (n=6), tumor progression concern (n=5), patient hesitation on treatment duration (n=3)

Theme 4: MDT Is Central but Not Universal

  • 60-80% of Stage III-IVA HNSCC patients discussed in MDT pre-surgery
  • 20-40% gap: patients with small tumors, oral cavity tumors, no lymph node involvement, or needing urgent surgery
  • MDT cadence is weekly or biweekly at most institutions

Theme 5: SubQ IO Is an Emerging Conversation

  • SubQ study kicked off November 2025 tracking QLEX conversations across NSCLC, melanoma, TNBC, RCC, urothelial
  • Early recruitment: 7 conversations collected (4 MD, 3 NP/PA) across melanoma, NSCLC, TNBC
  • Key question: Is SubQ being positioned as an "alternate" or the default?

5. Key Strategic Questions

  1. How fast will KN-689 adoption ramp? The ATU W1 baseline shows 70% of both ONCs and SURGs have moderate-to-high knowledge of KN-689, but only ~20-29% currently initiate NAT. The gap between knowledge and behavior is the key commercial challenge.

  2. Will PD-L1 testing become a barrier or accelerant? Reflexive testing adoption is trending positive, but implementation takes time (weeks to >1 year per institution). Community practices lag significantly.

  3. Can QLEX change the LOE math? If 30-40% of patients transition to QLEX by 2028, Merck retains a substantial protected revenue base. The April 2026 J-code is a critical inflection point.

  4. Where does HNSCC go from here? Perioperative is first; R/M halo effect, combination expansion (e.g., with cetuximab, ADCs), and potential HPV+ expansion are all possible vectors.

  5. What is the biosimilar defense timeline? IV biosimilar entry expected 2028-2029. Merck's patent estate, QLEX product hop, and indication-specific method-of-use patents create uncertainty about the speed and depth of erosion.

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home

1.1 KEYTRUDA Overview

Cervical cancer, gastric/GEJ, esophageal, hepatocellular, biliary tract, TNBC, MSI-H/dMMR tumors, and others

1L Metastatic: KEYTRUDA + chemotherapy (KEYNOTE-189 for nonsquamous; KEYNOTE-407 for squamous) is standard of care for PD-L1-expressing tumors. KEYTRUDA monotherapy is first-line for PD-L1 TPS >=50%
Neoadjuvant/Adjuvant: KEYNOTE-671 demonstrated EFS benefit for perioperative pembrolizumab + chemotherapy in resectable Stage II-IIIB NSCLC — approved March 2024

Patients focus on immediate surgery, not long-term treatment planning. MDs communicate at a 5th-grade reading level, and patients struggle to retain information

HNSCC — The New Frontier (KEYNOTE-689)

KEYNOTE-689: Phase 3 trial in 714 patients with resectable LA HNSCC (Stage III-IVA). Pembrolizumab as neoadjuvant treatment, continued as adjuvant with radiation +/- cisplatin, then as single agent

Primary endpoint (EFS): Median 51.8 months (pembro) vs. 30.4 months (SOC); HR 0.70

KEYNOTE-048 (R/M HNSCC): Pembrolizumab + chemotherapy or monotherapy (CPS >=1) for recurrent/metastatic — established IO standard of care since 2019

Urothelial Carcinoma — Padcev Combination

KEYNOTE-A39/EV-302: Pembrolizumab + enfortumab vedotin (Padcev) approved as 1L treatment for locally advanced or metastatic urothelial cancer. Also approved perioperatively for cisplatin-ineligible MIBC
This combination represents one of Keytruda's fastest-growing new indications

February 2026: FDA approved KEYTRUDA and KEYTRUDA QLEX + paclitaxel +/- bevacizumab for PD-L1+ (CPS >=1) platinum-resistant epithelial ovarian, fallopian tube, or primary peritoneal carcinoma — first and only PD-1 inhibitor approved in this setting

1.3 Subcutaneous Formulation: KEYTRUDA QLEX
KEYTRUDA QLEX (pembrolizumab and berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph) is the first and only subcutaneously administered immune checkpoint inhibitor:

Approved: September 2025 for adults across most solid tumor indications approved for KEYTRUDA
Administration: Can be given by a healthcare provider in as little as 1 minute vs. 30-minute IV infusion
Commercial launch: Late September 2025; Q4 2025 sales of $35 million ($40 million cumulative for 2025)
J-code: Permanent HCPCS code J9277 effective April 1, 2026, replacing temporary J9999 — this is expected to significantly accelerate adoption
Adoption target: Merck expects 30-40% of Keytruda patients to transition to QLEX by 2028
Strategic importance: QLEX is central to Merck's LOE defense strategy — the subcutaneous formulation carries its own patent estate, potentially shielding the franchise from IV biosimilar competition for years beyond 2028

2.2 HNSCC-Specific Competitive Landscape

Keytruda dominates 1L R/M with KEYNOTE-048 data (PD-L1 CPS >=1: pembro+chemo or pembro mono)

2.3 Biosimilar Competitive Landscape
Keytruda's composition-of-matter patent expires in 2028 in the US, opening the door for biosimilar entry:

Active biosimilar programs: Multiple companies including Samsung Bioepis, Biocon, Coherus, and others have pembrolizumab biosimilars in development
Expected biosimilar launch: Late 2028 to 2029 for IV formulations
Merck's patent estate defense: 50+ active US patents covering methods of use, formulations, and dosing regimens could protect aspects of Keytruda through 2042
QLEX as competitive moat: The subcutaneous formulation carries separate patents, and switching patients to QLEX before IV biosimilars enter creates a product-hop defense
Historical precedent: Biologics typically see 70-80% revenue erosion within 2-3 years of biosimilar entry, but Keytruda's breadth of indications and QLEX formulation may moderate this

5. Key Strategic Questions
